So my research project focuses on developing neuro-symbolic AI methods for fault detection in chemical processes. And you're probably wondering what that means exactly. So first of all, neuro-symbolic AI is something you've already been introduced to, so I'm not going to cover that. Then chemical processes, so the chemical process industry is one of the largest industrial sectors in the world and they produce many items from materials to customer goods for example pencils or there's different types of chemicals that you might use for example for cleaning And they take process safety very, very seriously.

And they have different types of studies that are applicable for different phases of a process. So planning is the phase before a chemical plant is built. And in construction as well, it's being built. Use is when it's being operated. And then end of life is for tearing down the plant. And the reason why they take chemical process safety so seriously is because there are lots of hazards involved.

So for example, gases that are at high temperature or high pressure, or substances that can be corrosive, toxic, flammable, or explosive. And they have a bunch of risk mitigation strategies and plans to avoid well to minimize the risk of these dangerous substances causing harm. FastLAS does not allow learning choices or preferences and has a limited ability to learn constant constraints.

But it's much more scalable. So these are extracts from the paper which show that here for the same task, it took ILASP 3 over 200 seconds and FastLAS only slightly under one second. And then similar here you can see a huge decrease in the amount of time to generate the optimal hypothesis. This one here, this task has a lower difference, but it's still a considerable improvements.

And more formally, though, it's an ILP system that takes as input a learning task and a user-specified scoring function. So what FastLoss does is that it computes an optimal hypothesis with respect to the scoring function. And a lower score is better. There are also proofs online. And the scoring function can be specified in multiple ways. So first, each training example has a weight associated.

And then you can also define additional mode bias rules and constraints. And these are given alongside the mode declarations. You can... So the first thing you can do is that you can assign a penalty to the rule heads and rule bodies that appear in a hypothesis. And I'm going to explain this a little bit more in detail later. And you can also eliminate rules from the hypothesis space.
And this is done via constraints in the bias.

for scoring candidate hypotheses.
